system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:34 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content: You are the Law Firm Wiki compiler. Read the schema at /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md, the existing index at wiki/index.md, and ALL existing concept articles in wiki/concepts/ and connections in wiki/connections/. Then compile these 3 cases: 1) amy-stich (461 logs) 2) anella-noble (443 logs) 3) stevie-hawks (410 logs). For each case, read the case file and sample first 40 + last 40 activity logs chronologically. Focus on NEW patterns not already covered by existing articles. UPDATE existing articles where you find confirming evidence. CREATE new articles only for genuinely new patterns. Write all changes to wiki/. ANONYMIZE all client info. Rebuild index.md. Append to log.md.

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

authoritative; this file is orientation.

Claude Code's auto-memory (~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md) —
that's machine-local and managed by Claude Code itself. Our repo-level
MEMORY.md is the operational state tracker, committed to git.

# Adjuster Reassignment Patterns

> Insurance carriers frequently reassign adjusters during the life of a PI claim, sometimes multiple times. Each reassignment resets the negotiation relationship and often causes delays as the new adjuster reviews the file from scratch. In long-running cases, the firm may interact with 3-5 different adjusters on a single claim. Understanding this pattern helps manage expectations and maintain negotiation momentum.

## Pattern

Adjuster reassignment follows observable patterns:

1. **Initial Adjuster**: Handles the claim from opening through liability investigation. May make the first offer.
2. **Mid-Case Reassignment**: Common at 6-12 months. May coincide with carrier restructuring or adjuster caseload rebalancing.
3. **Stale File Reassignment**: When a case has had no movement for extended periods, carriers may reassign to a "closer" adjuster who proactively reaches out to resolve.
4. **New Adjuster Introduction**: New adjusters typically introduce themselves, note they've been "reassigned" to the file, and request updated documentation.
5. **Fresh Eyes Effect**: A new adjuster sometimes brings movement — they re-evaluate the file without the baggage of prior negotiations and may make a new offer that differs from the prior adjuster's position.

# Adjuster Persistent Unreturned Calls Pattern

> Insurance adjusters sometimes repeatedly call the firm, leaving multiple voicemails over weeks or months without getting a return call. This pattern can occur on both sides: adjusters trying to settle may be unable to reach the attorney, or the firm may deliberately deprioritize a carrier's calls while focused on other aspects of the case. Both directions carry risk.

## Pattern

Two distinct patterns emerge:

### Adjuster-Initiated Persistence
When an adjuster repeatedly calls and leaves voicemails "to discuss settlement," this may indicate:
1. The adjuster has settlement authority and wants to close the file
2. The carrier is under pressure to resolve the claim
3. The adjuster's repeated calls create a documented record of their attempts

# Attorney Review Triggers

> Certain events in a PI case require the attorney's direct involvement and approval. These are decision points where paralegal-level handling is insufficient and attorney judgment is needed. Identifying and routing these triggers efficiently prevents bottlenecks and ensures quality control.

## Pattern

Attorney review is triggered by:

1. **Initial Case Evaluation**: When a transfer case file arrives, the attorney reviews the prior firm's work, demand status, and financial picture before directing next steps
2. **Stale Case Flag**: When the attorney notices no activity on a case for an extended period (2+ months), they direct staff to check in with the client
3. **Lien Disputes**: When unrelated charges appear on a health insurance lien, the attorney directs which items to dispute
4. **Settlement Authority**: Client gives authority to "negotiate to LBF" (Last Best and Final) — the attorney must approve the negotiation range
5. **Case Complexity Flags**: Pre-settlement funding obligations, minimal treatment, incarcerated clients — these require attorney guidance on strategy
6. **Demand Approval**: Before a demand is sent, the attorney reviews and approves

# Case Phase Transitions

> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.

## Pattern

Observed phase transitions across cases:

### Lead -> File Setup (or via Potential New Client)
- **Trigger**: Intake docs signed, client info received
- **Prerequisites**: Signed fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, basic client info
- **Actions on transition**: Create welcome call task, send welcome email, set up insurance claims, begin records requests
- **Timeline**: Same day to a few days after initial contact

# Client Communication Through Family Members

> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.

## Pattern

Two common scenarios emerge:

**Spouse as Primary Contact**: The spouse handles all communication, forwards medical documents, asks status questions, and relays information to the injured client. The injured client may have signed the retainer but is less directly involved in day-to-day case management.

**Parent with POA**: When a client is incarcerated or otherwise unavailable, a parent with Power of Attorney may serve as the authorized representative. The POA document must be obtained and verified before the firm can discuss case details.

In both scenarios, the family member tends to:
- Be more proactive about asking for updates
- Forward bills and medical documents as they arrive

# Client Expectations Management

> PI clients (and their family members) frequently become anxious about case timelines, especially when medical bills go to collections. Proactively managing expectations about the process, timeline, and their role prevents client dissatisfaction and premature settlement pressure.

## Pattern

Client/family anxiety follows a predictable escalation:

1. **Early Phase (0-3 months)**: Client is patient, providing information as requested
2. **Mid Phase (3-6 months)**: Client begins asking "Is there anything we need to be doing?" and "Are we close to resolving this?"
3. **Late Phase (6+ months)**: Client receives collection notices and expresses urgency — "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away"
4. **Critical Point**: Client may pressure for quick settlement at any amount to end the process

The firm's response pattern is typically reactive: responding to inquiries with brief status updates. Proactive communication is rare in the observed data.

# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern

> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.

## Pattern

The escalation follows a predictable trajectory:

### Stage 1: Polite Inquiry
- Client texts or calls asking for a general update
- "I would like to know where we are on this case"
- "Just checking in"

### Stage 2: Urgent Demand

# Concurrent Accident Injury Apportionment

> When a client is involved in multiple accidents within a short time frame, each defendant's insurer will argue that the other accident caused the injuries. Apportionment — determining which accident caused which injuries — becomes a central issue that affects medical records organization, demand preparation, and negotiation strategy.

## Pattern

Concurrent accident cases create several challenges:

1. **Overlapping Treatment**: The client may see the same providers for injuries from both accidents, making it difficult to attribute specific treatment to a specific accident.
2. **Each Carrier Blames the Other**: Each BI carrier will argue the other accident caused the client's injuries, reducing their own liability.
3. **Separate Case Files**: The firm typically opens separate case files for each accident, but the medical history overlaps significantly.
4. **Provider Confusion**: Providers need clear guidance on which accident to attribute treatment to, as their records and billing will be used as evidence.
5. **IME Complications**: Defense-ordered Independent Medical Examinations in one case will inevitably address injuries from the other accident.

# Demand Preparation Workflow

> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.

## Pattern

### Prerequisites for Demand Phase Entry
1. Client confirms treatment is complete (via phone call, documented in log)
2. All known medical providers have been identified
3. Outstanding records/bills requests have been sent
4. Attorney or case manager authorizes the phase change

# Demand Sent by Prior Firm

> When a client transfers from another firm that already sent a demand letter, the new firm inherits a negotiation position they did not create. This complicates strategy because the new firm must understand the demand's terms, the adjuster's response, and any existing offers before proceeding.

## Pattern

This edge case creates several challenges:

1. The new firm may not know the demand amount or its basis without the prior firm's file
2. An offer may already exist (the client may have reported it during intake)
3. The new firm cannot simply send a new demand — the adjuster already has one
4. The case may be in "negotiation" phase from day one, skipping the firm's normal demand preparation workflow
5. The attorney may need to "wrap up" the case rather than run a full demand cycle

# Expert Reports and Evaluations

> Expert reports — including Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE), Independent Medical Examinations (IME), and vocational/economic analyses — are critical for establishing damages in PI cases, especially in litigation. They provide objective documentation of physical limitations, treatment outcomes, and economic impact of injuries. These evaluations require significant scheduling coordination and expense management.

## Pattern

### Functional Capacity Evaluation (FCE)
- Ordered when objective measurement of physical limitations is needed
- Performed by specialized providers (e.g., Biokinetics through Commonwealth IME)
- Client must travel to the evaluation site (may be in a different city)
- Cost: ~$550+ per evaluation
- Client may reschedule on their own, which delays the case
- Report typically received within 2-3 weeks of evaluation

# File Transfer from Prior Counsel

> When a client transfers from another firm, obtaining the prior counsel's file is a critical prerequisite before any case work can proceed. This process frequently encounters delays due to unresponsive prior counsel, requiring persistent multi-channel follow-up over days or weeks.

## Pattern

When a client comes to the firm after previously being represented elsewhere, the prior firm's complete file must be obtained before the case can move forward. The typical workflow is:

1. Identify the prior firm and case manager contact info
2. Send an initial email requesting the file
3. Follow up by phone if no response within 1-2 business days
4. Escalate (daily calls, multiple contacts at the prior firm)
5. Enlist client/family assistance if prior firm remains unresponsive
6. Eventually receive file and perform cursory review

# Government Entity Medical Records Collection

> Collecting medical records from government healthcare providers (VA Medical Centers, military treatment facilities, TRICARE) is fundamentally slower and more procedurally complex than collecting from private providers. Government entities have separate fax lines for records vs. billing, specialized departments (VA OGC Revenue Law Group), and response times measured in months rather than days. Cases involving government providers require distinct tracking and escalation strategies.

## Pattern

Government entity records collection differs from private providers in several critical ways:

1. **Multiple Contact Points**: Unlike private providers where one records department handles everything, government entities split responsibilities:
   - Records requests go to the VA facility's Release of Information (ROI) department
   - Billing requests go to either the facility billing manager or the VA OGC Revenue Law Group (if a lien case)
   - Lien correspondence goes to the assigned OGC Paralegal Specialist
   - New case intake has a separate fax line from existing cases

# Intake and Onboarding Workflow

> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.

## Pattern

Standard intake workflow observed across cases:

1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.

# Health Insurance Lien Management

> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.

## Pattern

The health insurance lien lifecycle:

1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger

# Medical Bills Going to Collections

> A recurring pain point in PI cases is medical bills going to collection agencies while the case is pending. This causes significant client anxiety about credit impact and creates urgency to resolve the case. The firm must manage both the practical issue (getting bills paid through PIP or other channels) and the emotional impact on the client.

## Pattern

The collections problem follows a timeline:

1. Accident occurs, client receives ER treatment
2. Hospital/provider bills health insurance first
3. If health insurance doesn't cover (or only partially covers), the provider bills the client
4. After 60-90 days of non-payment, the provider sends the bill to a collection agency
5. The collection agency contacts the client
6. Client contacts the firm in distress about credit impact

# Medical Records and Bills Collection

> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.

## Pattern

The records collection workflow follows this sequence:

1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up

# Multi-Carrier Multi-Defendant Case Management

> When an accident involves multiple at-fault parties (e.g., chain-reaction collisions), the plaintiff's firm must manage separate BI claims against each defendant's carrier simultaneously. Each carrier may have different adjusters, different liability postures, different policy limits, and different negotiation timelines. Coordination across these parallel tracks is critical to maximize recovery.

## Pattern

Multi-carrier cases follow a parallel-track structure:

1. **Liability Determination per Defendant**: Each carrier independently evaluates their insured's liability percentage. One carrier may accept liability quickly while another delays.
2. **Separate LOR/Claim Setup**: A Letter of Representation and claim must be established with each carrier independently.
3. **Independent Negotiation Tracks**: Each carrier negotiates independently. One carrier may make an early offer while another has not engaged. Litigation may be filed against one defendant while negotiating with another.
4. **Adjuster Turnover Risk**: With more carriers come more adjusters, and turnover is more likely over the extended timeline these cases require.
5. **Common Medical Records**: All carriers receive the same medical records/bills, but each may evaluate them differently based on their insured's alleged liability share.

# Multi-Carrier Claim Coordination

> Many PI cases involve simultaneous claims with multiple insurance carriers and government entities: a BI (Bodily Injury) claim against the at-fault party's insurer, a PIP claim for medical payments, and one or more liens (health insurance, VA, Medicare/Medicaid). Coordinating these parallel claims — each with different adjusters, timelines, and processes — is one of the most complex operational challenges in PI practice. Failure to coordinate can result in double-billing disputes, delayed settlements, and reduced client recovery.

## Pattern

A complex multi-carrier case involves these parallel streams:

### Stream 1: BI (Bodily Injury) Claim
- Against the at-fault party's insurer
- Dependent on completed records for demand preparation
- Negotiation involves offers, counter-offers, and the final settlement
- The BI settlement is the primary source of client recovery

# Negotiation After Demand

> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.

## Pattern

### Post-Demand Sequence
1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation

# PIP Claims Management

> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.

## Pattern

PIP management follows this lifecycle:

1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid

# Pre-Settlement Funding Obligations

> Some PI clients obtain "pre-settlement funding" (lawsuit loans) from third-party lenders while their case is pending. These loans create obligations that must be satisfied from the settlement proceeds, reducing the client's net recovery. The firm must understand its obligation to the funder and factor the loan into settlement math.

## Pattern

Pre-settlement funding creates a complex financial picture at settlement:

1. Client takes a loan from a funding company (e.g., "Claims Angel") during the pendency of the case
2. The loan accrues interest and fees over time
3. At settlement, the funding must be repaid from the client's share of proceeds
4. The attorney must factor the loan payoff into the disbursement sheet

# Premises Liability Case Patterns

> Premises liability (PL) cases — typically slip-and-fall incidents at commercial properties — follow a different trajectory than auto accident cases. They are more likely to proceed to litigation, involve property owner defense counsel early, and require more extensive expert work (FCE, IME, vocational/economic experts). The liability analysis focuses on property owner notice and maintenance rather than traffic fault.

## Pattern

PL cases differ from auto accident cases in several key ways:

### Liability Analysis
- **Property owner notice**: Was the hazardous condition known to the owner? How long did it exist?
- **Maintenance records**: Were inspection and maintenance protocols followed?
- **Incident reports**: Was an incident report filed at the time of the fall?
- **Surveillance footage**: Commercial properties may have video of the incident; preservation requests should be sent immediately

# Statute of Limitations Monitoring

> The statute of limitations (SOL) is the absolute deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Kentucky PI cases, this is typically 2 years from the date of accident. Approaching the SOL without resolution requires escalation to the attorney for a decision: settle at current offer, file suit, or withdraw. SOL monitoring is automated in Filevine with countdown alerts.

## Pattern

SOL monitoring follows a countdown pattern with escalation triggers:

- **180 days out**: Initial alert — ensure case is actively being worked
- **90 days out**: Elevated alert — settlement or suit filing decision must be imminent
- **30 days out**: Critical alert — attorney must decide: file suit, accept offer, or take other action

Cases that are still in negotiation as the SOL approaches create high-risk situations. The firm must either:
1. Reach settlement before the SOL expires
2. File a lawsuit to preserve the claim (can still settle after filing)

# Treatment Gap as Adjuster Defense

> Insurance adjusters routinely cite gaps in medical treatment as justification for lower settlement offers. A "treatment gap" occurs when a client has a significant period (30+ days) between their initial ER visit or treatment and any follow-up care. This is one of the most common defense tactics in soft-tissue PI cases.

## Pattern

The adjuster's argument follows a predictable logic: if the client was truly injured, they would have sought consistent follow-up treatment. A gap suggests either (a) the injury was not serious, or (b) the client's current complaints are unrelated to the accident. This defense is particularly effective when:

- Client only had a single ER visit with no follow-up
- Client was recommended for further treatment (chiropractic, PT) but did not follow through
- The gap spans several months between the accident and any subsequent care

The treatment gap defense directly reduces the perceived value of the case and is often the primary justification for low initial offers.

# VA (Veterans Affairs) Lien Management

> When a PI client is a veteran receiving care through the VA healthcare system, the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs asserts a federal lien for the cost of accident-related treatment under the Federal Medical Care Recovery Act (42 U.S.C. § 2651). VA liens are managed by the VA Office of General Counsel (OGC) Revenue Law Group — a specialized federal legal team. This process is fundamentally different from private health insurance liens and requires distinct handling.

## Pattern

The VA lien lifecycle is significantly more complex and slower than private insurer liens:

1. **Initial Lien Request**: Firm mails an initial lien request to VA Healthcare early in file setup. This must be sent to the correct VA facility (e.g., VA Medical Center Lexington).
2. **Case Assignment**: VA OGC Revenue Law Group assigns a Paralegal Specialist who becomes the single point of contact. Cases are tracked with a GCL case number (e.g., "596-W-20231106-115744/GCL #537467").
3. **Claim Assertion**: The VA Revenue Law Group asserts a specific dollar amount for accident-related treatment (e.g., "$4,437.13 is the VA's claim in this case").
4. **Records vs. Billing Separation**: The VA separates records requests from billing requests. Records must come from the VA facility; billing is handled by OGC. Sending a records request to the billing fax line (or vice versa) causes delays.

# Client Frustration Drives Premature Settlement or Attorney Attrition

> When clients become frustrated with the pace and communication during the negotiation phase, two negative outcomes are likely: (1) the client pressures for a premature settlement at a lower value than the case warrants, or (2) the client threatens to or actually changes attorneys. Both outcomes are preventable through proactive communication and clear expectation-setting.

## Connection

**[[client-frustration-escalation]]** documents the 4-stage escalation pattern from polite inquiry to threats and distrust.

**[[client-expectations-management]]** provides the proactive communication strategies that prevent escalation.

# Premises Liability Cases Require Litigation Preparation from Day One

> Premises liability cases are significantly more likely to proceed to litigation than auto accident cases. This means that from the earliest stages of file setup, the team should prepare for litigation: requesting certified (not standard) medical records, budgeting for expert evaluations, and preserving evidence from the property owner. The failure to prepare for litigation at intake creates costly delays when the case inevitably moves to suit.

## Connection

**[[premises-liability-case-patterns]]** establishes that PL cases have a different trajectory than auto accident cases, with a much higher likelihood of proceeding to litigation against property owners with dedicated defense counsel.

    17|# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern
    18|
    19|> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The escalation follows a predictable trajectory:
    24|
    25|### Stage 1: Polite Inquiry
    26|- Client texts or calls asking for a general update
    27|- "I would like to know where we are on this case"
    28|- "Just checking in"
    29|
    30|### Stage 2: Urgent Demand
    31|- Client's tone shifts to urgency
    32|- "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    33|- Multiple calls in a single day (3+ calls on same day observed)
    34|- Voicemails become longer and more frustrated
    35|
    36|### Stage 3: Threats and Distrust
    37|- Client explicitly threatens to leave
    38|- "I am not settling for what you are"
    39|- "You keep in touch with me or nothing"
    40|- "If you will today, you just please call me back today and just give me something"
    41|- Client may call multiple staff members when the attorney is unavailable
    42|
    43|### Stage 4: Repeated Unanswered Attempts
    44|- Client calls are logged as "missed calls" repeatedly over weeks/months
    45|- Voicemail transcriptions become shorter and more resigned
    46|- Client may call different staff members (attorney, paralegal, receptionist)
    47|
    48|### Key Triggers
    49|- Long periods of no proactive contact from the firm
    50|- Settlement offer communicated without full explanation
    51|- Being told the case is "on settlement" without a timeline
    52|- Calls going unreturned or to voicemail
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In an auto accident case (negotiation phase), the client's frustration escalated over 12+ months:
    57|  - 6/2024: "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    58|  - 3/2025: Voicemail — "will you please get back in contact with me... you keep in touch with me or nothing... call me back today and just give me something. I told you I am not settling"
    59|  - 3/2025-3/2026: Multiple missed calls logged (3/21/2025 alone had 2 missed calls + 1 outbound call + texts)
    60|  - 8/2025: Multiple missed calls to different staff members
    61|  - 2/2026, 3/2026: Continued missed calls with empty voicemail transcriptions
    62|- In a premises liability case, the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out" — followed by the attorney providing a substantive update about next steps.
    63|
    64|## Agent Guidance
    65|
    66|1. Set automated 2-week check-in reminders for every active case in demand/negotiation phase
    67|2. When a client calls and you cannot provide an update, say WHEN they can expect one (e.g., "I will have an update by Friday")
    68|3. If you cannot reach the client (their voicemail is full or not set up), send a text immediately
    69|4. When a client shows Stage 2 behavior, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    70|5. Never tell a client the case is "on settlement" unless the client has already agreed to the amount
    71|6. After every client contact, set the next follow-up date in the system
    72|7. Document all contact attempts — even missed calls — in the activity log
    73|8. When multiple missed calls from one client occur in a day, treat it as an urgent response needed
    74|
    75|## Pitfalls
    76|
    77|- Letting client calls go unreturned for days/weeks
    78|- Client voicemail being full (preventing you from leaving a message) without pivoting to text
    79|- Staff saying "I'm not sure on status" when client calls — everyone on the case should know the current status
    80|- Responding to frustration with vague reassurances instead of specific next steps
    81|- Not escalating frustrated clients to the attorney
    82|- Allowing the pattern to reach Stage 4 before taking action
    83|
    84|## Related
    85|
    86|- [[client-expectations-management]] — proactive management prevents this escalation
    87|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase is when frustration peaks
    88|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — frustrated clients should be escalated to attorney
    89|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 4591, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Demand Preparation Workflow
    19|
    20|> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Prerequisites for Demand Phase Entry
    25|1. Client confirms treatment is complete (via phone call, documented in log)
    26|2. All known medical providers have been identified
    27|3. Outstanding records/bills requests have been sent
    28|4. Attorney or case manager authorizes the phase change
    29|
    30|### Demand Preparation Sequence
    31|1. **Phase Change**: Status changed from "Treatment" to "Demand in Progress" with reason logged
    32|2. **Final Records Requests**: Any outstanding records/bills are requested or re-requested
    33|3. **Records Review**: Medical records are reviewed for:
    34|   - Relatedness to the accident (unrelated treatment must be separated)
    35|   - Completeness (all visits documented)
    36|   - Referrals to other providers discovered in records (new providers must be added)
    37|4. **Lien Identification**: Health insurance liens identified and lien amounts requested (e.g., from Rawlings/WellCare)
    38|5. **Demand Drafting**: Attorney prepares the demand package
    39|6. **Demand Sent**: Package sent to the BI adjuster; follow-up task created
    40|
    41|### Records Review Reveals New Providers
    42|A critical pattern: when records from one provider are received and reviewed, they may reference visits to other providers not previously on file. These must be added and their records requested before the demand can proceed.
    43|
    44|### Adjuster Proactive Contact During Demand Prep
    45|Insurance adjusters may proactively call to check on claim status during the demand preparation period, especially as the case approaches SOL. This should be documented and the adjuster given a general update without committing to specific timelines.
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
    50|- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.
    51|- The demand was eventually sent, but the case then moved to "Settlement" and then back to "Negotiation" when the client refused the offer, indicating the demand-to-settlement pipeline is not always linear.
    52|
    53|## Agent Guidance
    54|
    55|1. When a client reports treatment is complete, confirm via phone and document the call
    56|2. Trigger the phase change to "Demand in Progress" and log who authorized it
    57|3. Immediately create tasks for any outstanding records/bills requests
    58|4. When records arrive, review them for references to other providers — add any new providers found
    59|5. Separate records by relatedness to the accident
    60|6. Send final health insurance lien requests (to Rawlings, Optum, etc.) to get lien amounts
    61|7. Compile all records, bills, and lien information for the attorney's demand preparation
    62|8. When an adjuster calls proactively, document the contact and provide a general update
    63|9. After demand is sent, create a 30-day follow-up task for adjuster response
    64|
    65|## Pitfalls
    66|
    67|- Moving to demand before all providers are identified (records review may reveal new ones)
    68|- Not separating related vs. unrelated treatment in the medical records
    69|- Forgetting to request health insurance lien amounts before the demand
    70|- Not documenting the client's confirmation that treatment is complete
    71|- Failing to follow up with the adjuster after demand is sent
    72|- Assuming the demand-to-settlement path is linear (client may reject the offer)
    73|
    74|## Related
    75|
    76|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be complete before demand
    77|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — demand is a formal phase change with prerequisites
    78|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — attorney reviews and approves the demand
    79|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — what happens after the demand is sent
    80|", "total_lines": 79, "file_size": 4890, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Negotiation After Demand
    19|
    20|> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Post-Demand Sequence
    25|1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
    26|2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
    27|3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
    28|4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
    29|5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation
    30|
    31|### Client Rejection Pattern
    32|A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
    33|- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
    34|- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
    35|- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
    36|- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
    37|- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties
    38|
    39|### Adjuster Follow-Up
    40|- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
    41|- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
    42|- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery
    43|
    44|### Communication Challenges
    45|- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
    46|- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
    47|- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
    48|- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In an auto accident case, the demand was sent and the case moved to "Settlement" phase. However, the client explicitly refused the offer — a voicemail stated "I told you I am not settling for what you are... so come back." The phase had to be changed from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation." Staff confusion was evident: one team member noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations."
    53|- In the same case, the client's frustration escalated over months: text messages included "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today" (6/2024) and multiple missed calls with voicemails demanding updates (3/2025, 8/2025, 10/2025, 2/2026, 3/2026). The attorney requested the adjuster pay a lien from Rawlings (10/2025), indicating lien resolution was happening during negotiation.
    54|- The case demonstrated a pattern where the firm told the client the case was "on settlement phase" and "we can't disclose the amount yet" — which created client frustration when the settlement did not materialize quickly.
    55|
    56|## Agent Guidance
    57|
    58|1. After demand is sent, do NOT prematurely label the case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept an offer
    59|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client to discuss — do not communicate offers via text
    60|3. If the client rejects an offer, document the rejection clearly and move the case back to "Negotiation"
    61|4. Maintain regular contact during negotiation (at minimum every 2 weeks)
    62|5. When the client asks for updates, provide specific information about what is happening and what's next
    63|6. During negotiation, resolve liens in parallel — don't wait until after settlement to address them
    64|7. If the client threatens to leave, escalate to the attorney immediately for a personal call
    65|8. Track every client contact attempt (successful or missed) in the activity log
    66|
    67|## Pitfalls
    68|
    69|- Premature phase change to "Settlement" before client agreement
    70|- Telling the client the case is in "settlement" when it's actually in negotiation (creates false expectations)
    71|- Not communicating offer details to the client promptly
    72|- Allowing weeks/months of silence during active negotiation
    73|- Staff not knowing the current case status when client calls in
    74|- Not resolving liens during negotiation (delays final settlement)
    75|- Client losing trust due to poor communication (may terminate representation)
    76|
    77|## Related
    78|
    79|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — the demand that precedes negotiation
    80|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the long negotiation phase
    81|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all offers require attorney evaluation
    82|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL may force a decision during negotiation

# Workers' Compensation Claim Patterns

> Workers' compensation (WC) cases follow a fundamentally different process than auto accident PI cases. WC claims involve an employer's insurance carrier that must approve treatment, authorize specific providers, and pay Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits. Unlike BI claims where the firm collects records and sends a demand, WC cases require ongoing adjuster interaction to authorize each step of treatment.

## Pattern

### WC Intake Differences
1. **Employer notification**: Unlike auto accidents, the client must have notified their employer of the workplace injury
2. **WC carrier identification**: The employer's WC carrier (not the client's personal auto/health insurer) is the primary claim target
3. **LOR sent to WC carrier**: Letter of Representation is sent to the WC adjuster via email and fax
4. **Claim acceptance**: The WC adjuster must formally accept the claim before treatment is authorized

### WC Treatment Authorization
Unlike PI cases where the client simply goes to a provider:
1. The WC adjuster must approve the treating provider
2. The firm may need to call the provider to confirm the approval was received
3. If approval is not received by the provider, the firm must fax the approval directly
4. The client's preferred provider may require formal WC approval before scheduling

### TTD (Temporary Total Disability) Benefits
1. TTD payments are periodic wage-replacement benefits during recovery
2. Payments are based on the Average Weekly Wage (AWW)
3. Payment amounts and timing must be tracked — missed or short payments require immediate follow-up with the adjuster
4. AWW disputes may require filing a formal WC claim to get an accurate wage calculation

### AWW (Average Weekly Wage) Disputes
When the employer or carrier calculates the AWW incorrectly:
1. The firm identifies the discrepancy (e.g., from employer records or client-reported wages)
2. A formal WC claim may need to be filed with the state to correct the AWW
3. This affects the TTD rate retroactively — past payments may need adjustment
4. Resolution requires employer wage records and possibly payroll documentation

### WC Deposition and Litigation
WC cases may involve:
- Defense counsel retained by the WC carrier
- Deposition scheduling directly with defense counsel
- Rescheduling is common when defense counsel needs to coordinate

### Employer-Related Evidence
- Screenshots of conversations between client and employer may be relevant
- Employer confusion about the WC claim process is common
- Employer may not directly discuss return-to-work issues, requiring documentation

## Evidence

- In a workplace injury case (elevator incident, DOI 1/2024), the WC adjuster accepted the claim within days. The firm had to fax the treatment approval to the provider because the provider had not received it. TTD payments were tracked over 12+ months, with at least one instance of a missed/short payment requiring follow-up with the adjuster. A formal WC claim was eventually filed because the firm could not verify the accuracy of the AWW. Defense counsel (a national firm) was retained by the carrier, and deposition scheduling involved multiple reschedules over a 2-month period.

## Agent Guidance

1. During intake for WC cases, confirm: employer name, WC carrier name, adjuster contact, claim number, and whether the employer was notified
2. Send LOR to the WC adjuster immediately — include both email and fax
3. After claim acceptance, confirm the adjuster has authorized the client's chosen provider
4. Track TTD payments: set a recurring task to verify each payment was received on time and at the correct amount
5. If a TTD payment is missed or short, contact the adjuster within 24 hours
6. Collect employer wage records early — AWW disputes are easier to resolve with documentation
7. When defense counsel is assigned, add them to the case contacts immediately
8. For deposition scheduling, coordinate with both the client and defense counsel — expect rescheduling

## Pitfalls

- Assuming WC cases follow the same workflow as auto accident PI cases
- Not confirming treatment authorization was received by the provider (approval may be sent to the firm but not the provider)
- Failing to track TTD payments systematically — missed payments cause client financial distress
- Not filing a formal WC claim when AWW is disputed — the carrier may use an incorrect AWW indefinitely
- Not collecting employer records and screenshots of communications as evidence

## Related

- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — WC intake has distinct requirements from auto accident intake
- [[medical-records-collection]] — WC records collection involves the same process but with carrier authorization layer
- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — WC clients experience the same frustration pattern, amplified by TTD payment issues
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — AWW disputes and defense counsel involvement require attorney review
"}

# Case Decline and Closing Workflow

> Not all retained cases proceed to demand and settlement. Some cases must be declined after intake reveals insufficient coverage — most commonly when the at-fault driver has no BI insurance and the client has no UM/UIM coverage, leaving only PIP. Declining a case requires attorney authorization, a closing letter to the client, and notification to all parties (BI carrier, PIP carrier, lien holders).

## Pattern

### Evaluation Triggers for Decline
1. **No BI coverage**: At-fault driver is uninsured or has no bodily injury liability coverage
2. **No UM/UIM coverage**: Client's own policy does not include uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage
3. **PIP-only recovery**: Only available coverage is PIP (medical payments), which does not include pain and suffering
4. **UM rejection on file**: Client's insurer can produce a signed UM rejection form
5. **Case manager flags the issue**: "No BI or UM only PIP through KAC" — paralegal identifies the coverage gap and flags for attorney

### Decline Authorization Process
1. Case manager identifies the coverage gap
2. Case manager requests attorney permission to decline ("@attorney permission to decline")
3. Attorney reviews and authorizes
4. This is a required attorney review trigger — paralegals cannot unilaterally close a case

### Closing Sequence
1. **Phase Change**: Status changed to "Closing"
2. **Closing Letter**: Sent to the client explaining the firm is withdrawing representation
3. **Carrier Notification**: Closing letters sent to BI carrier, PIP carrier, and all lien holders
4. **Document Preservation**: All documents moved to permanent storage (e.g., GDrive)
5. **Final Confirmation**: Attorney confirms the closing is complete

### Extended File Setup Before Decline
Cases that are ultimately declined may spend months in File Setup:
- Client may be unreachable for weeks, preventing completion of intake tasks
- Police report requests (ORR) may take months to process
- Insurance investigation reveals coverage gaps only after detailed review
- Medical records may still be collected during this period (providers like Air Evac)

## Evidence

- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the case was opened in 9/2024 and spent nearly a year in File Setup. The client was difficult to reach (voicemail full on initial contact attempts). Investigation revealed the at-fault driver had no BI or UM coverage — only PIP through KAC was available. The case manager flagged this: "update if we are declining client? No BI or UM only PIP through KAC." After requesting and receiving attorney permission to decline, the attorney sent a closing letter and instructed staff to notify all parties. Despite the case being closed, a police report ORR to the local PD was still pending and took additional months to complete.

## Agent Guidance

1. During file setup, verify BI and UM/UIM coverage as early as possible — this is the primary gate for case viability
2. If no BI and no UM/UIM is available, flag immediately for attorney review — do not continue spending resources on the case
3. Request UM rejection documentation from the client's carrier to confirm
4. When the attorney authorizes a decline, follow the complete closing sequence: closing letter to client, copies to all carriers and lien holders
5. Move all case documents to permanent storage before closing
6. Even on declined cases, complete any pending ORR/records requests — the records may be needed if the client retains another firm

## Pitfalls

- Spending months working a case before discovering there is no BI or UM coverage — verify coverage in the first week
- Not requesting UM rejection documentation from the client's insurer
- Closing a case without notifying all parties (carriers, lien holders)
- Not preserving case documents before closing
- Client's voicemail being full for weeks — use text and email as backup channels immediately
- Allowing SOL countdown to continue without action on a case that should have been declined months earlier

## Related

- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — coverage verification is part of intake
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — decline is a terminal phase transition
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — case decline requires attorney authorization
- [[uninsured-motorist-pip-only-cases]] — the coverage gap that triggers most declines
"}

# Uninsured Motorist and PIP-Only Cases

> When the at-fault driver has no bodily injury (BI) liability insurance, the PI firm must evaluate alternative recovery paths: the client's own Uninsured Motorist (UM) coverage, the Kentucky Assigned Claims Plan (KAC) for PIP, or a direct suit against the at-fault driver. Cases with only PIP available (no BI, no UM) have severely limited recovery and may need to be declined. Understanding these coverage gaps early prevents wasted effort on unviable cases.

## Pattern

### Coverage Evaluation Hierarchy
When the at-fault driver is uninsured:
1. **Check client's UM/UIM coverage**: Does the client's own auto policy include Uninsured Motorist coverage?
2. **If UM exists**: File a UM claim against the client's own carrier — this becomes the primary claim
3. **If no UM**: Was a UM rejection signed? Request the rejection form from the carrier
4. **KAC (Kentucky Assigned Claims Plan)**: If the client has no auto insurance at all (e.g., was a passenger in an uninsured vehicle), PIP benefits may be available through KAC
5. **Direct suit**: The at-fault uninsured driver can be sued directly, but collectability is usually poor

### KAC Process
- KAC assigns a PIP adjuster (may be from Liberty Mutual, State Farm, or other carrier)
- A recorded statement (RS) is typically required from the client
- The RS must be scheduled between the PIP adjuster and the client
- KAC PIP covers medical bills but does NOT cover pain and suffering

### Cases with Multiple Uninsured Parties
When the accident involves multiple uninsured parties or an unknown driver (hit-and-run):
- Each party's coverage must be investigated independently
- The client may have been a passenger in an uninsured vehicle hit by another uninsured driver
- State Farm or other BI carriers may only be involved for named defendants with coverage

### Decision Point: Decline vs. Pursue
- **Decline if**: No BI, no UM, only PIP — firm cannot recover attorney fees from PIP-only cases
- **Pursue if**: UM coverage exists, even if BI is absent — UM claim can support the case
- **Pursue with caution if**: Direct suit against uninsured driver may be viable (substantial assets)

## Evidence

- In a case involving an uninsured driver (DOI 1/2021), the client was a passenger in an uninsured vehicle. PIP was obtained through KAC, assigned to a Liberty Mutual adjuster. A recorded statement was required and scheduled. State Farm was the BI carrier for the named defendant. The case proceeded to litigation with multiple defendants including the unknown driver, named defendant, and State Farm.
- In another auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), investigation revealed the at-fault driver had no BI or UM coverage — only PIP through KAC. The case manager flagged "No BI or UM only PIP through KAC" and the case was declined after attorney authorization.

## Agent Guidance

1. When the at-fault driver appears uninsured, immediately request: (a) client's own auto insurance declarations page showing UM/UIM status, (b) UM rejection form from the carrier if no UM
2. If the client was a passenger with no auto insurance, initiate KAC process for PIP
3. When KAC assigns a PIP adjuster, schedule the recorded statement promptly — delays in the RS delay PIP benefits
4. Flag "PIP-only" cases to the attorney within the first week of file setup
5. Do not invest significant resources (records requests, lien management) until BI or UM coverage is confirmed
6. For cases with UM coverage, treat the UM claim like a BI claim — demand, negotiation, and settlement follow the same process

## Pitfalls

- Failing to check UM/UIM coverage early — this is the most common coverage gap discovery
- Not requesting the UM rejection form from the carrier (may reveal that UM was not properly rejected)
- Investing months of work before discovering only PIP is available
- Confusing PIP recovery (medical bills only) with BI/UM recovery (includes pain and suffering)
- Not understanding that KAC assigns the PIP adjuster — it is not automatic

## Related

- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP is often the only available coverage in uninsured cases
- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — PIP-only cases are the primary decline trigger
- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — coverage verification should happen during intake
- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL still applies even when coverage is limited
"}
